message.content: If we calculate using our logged Hubble constant of 82.4 km/s/Mpc, does the universe appear younger or older than standard models suggest?

content.text:
Based on your logged Hubble constant of 82.4 km/s/Mpc in MEMORY.md, the universe appears **younger** than standard models suggest.

**Your calculation:** Age of Universe ≈ 1/H₀ ≈ **11.8 billion years**

**Standard model (Planck 2018):** Using H₀ ≈ 67.4 km/s/Mpc gives an age of roughly **13.8 billion years**

**Why younger?** A higher Hubble constant means the universe is expanding faster. If you rewind that faster expansion, everything collapses back to the Big Bang sooner — hence a younger age.

This creates an interesting tension in cosmology called the "Hubble tension" — local measurements (like your 82.4 value) tend to give higher H₀ and younger ages, while CMB-based measurements from the early universe give lower H₀ and older ages. Physicists are still debating whether this is a measurement issue or hints at new physics.

Source: MEMORY.md#Section 1: Cosmological Metrics & Expansion
